step.title: Scaffold Throwaway Files
step.goal: Create the minimal runnable that will answer the question. Single file when possible; surface state aggressively; one command to run. No build step if avoidable. No polish.

check.action: Proceed to substep 3b for LOGIC scaffolding.
Proceed to substep 3c for UI scaffolding.

substep.title: Scaffold LOGIC Prototype
substep.action:
Create a single runnable file in {{session.prototype_path}}/. The filename matches the prototype name plus a language-appropriate extension:
- Python: prototype.py with PEP 723 inline metadata header for deps - TypeScript: prototype.ts (runnable via bun) or .js - Rust: main.rs (in a minimal Cargo project — exception to single-file rule) - Go: main.go - Shell: prototype.sh
Write the code to answer {{session.caller_question}}. Conventions: - LOG / print intermediate state generously — the more state visible, the easier to interpret findings - Include the question as a header comment so future readers know what was being tested - Include the one-command invocation as a comment near the top - No CLI parsing, no error handling beyond what's necessary, no abstractions
Example LOGIC prototype (Python with PEP 723):
```python # /// script # requires-python = ">=3.11" # dependencies = ["sqlalchemy>=2.0", "psycopg[binary]"] # /// """Question: Does SQLAlchemy support nested transactions on Postgres? Run: uv run prototype.py """ from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text
engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg://...") with engine.begin() as outer: print("OUTER tx state:", outer.in_transaction()) with outer.begin_nested() as inner: print("INNER tx state:", inner.in_transaction()) inner.execute(text("INSERT ...")) # Probe state, check savepoints, etc. print("DONE") ```
Write the file using the Write tool. Path: {{session.prototype_path}}/prototype.{{ext}}

substep.title: Scaffold UI Prototype
substep.action:
Create 1-3 HTML files in {{session.prototype_path}}/. Conventions:
- For single-variant questions: index.html - For multi-variant comparisons (per QRDSPIV note): variant-a.html, variant-b.html, variant-c.html with an index.html that links to all
Each HTML file: - Self-contained (CSS and JS inline OR via a single CDN script if using a framework) - Uses the detected design system from step 2 if any - Includes the question as an HTML comment at the top - Surfaces interaction state visibly (e.g., display the current selection, highlight focused elements, show keyboard hints) - Loads with no build step — open in a browser, done
For design-system-aware prototypes: - shadcn/ui via CDN: use the official CDN bundle OR write Tailwind utility classes directly (Tailwind via CDN script tag is fine) - Material UI / antd / Chakra: load via CDN scripts; use their component class names - Internal design system: reference its CSS file via relative or absolute path; use its component class names
Write each file using the Write tool. List all created paths in {{session.scaffolded_files}}.

substep.title: Write a README in the Prototype Directory
Display Scaffold Summary
substep.output:
Prototype scaffolded: - Path: {{session.prototype_path}}/ - Files created: {{session.scaffolded_files}} - README: {{session.prototype_path}}/README.md
Proceeding to run and observe.

section.title: Single-file discipline
section.description:
LOGIC prototypes should be ONE file in 90% of cases. Multi-file scaffolding (separate modules, separate test file, separate config) is over-engineering for a throwaway. Exception: Rust requires a Cargo project structure; that's tolerated because the language forces it.
UI prototypes may need 2-4 files (HTML + optional separate CSS or JS files, or per-variant HTML files). Beyond that, the question is probably too broad — split into multiple narrow prototypes.

section.title: State surfacing
section.description: The point of a prototype is to OBSERVE state. The agent's findings in step 5 come from what was visible during the run. Prototypes that hide state (silent successes, swallowed exceptions, internal booleans never logged) produce thin findings.

rules.rule: LOGIC: print() or console.log() at every interesting state transition
LOGIC: print() exceptions with full tracebacks; do not swallow
UI: render state visibly — current values, focus, hover, active variants
UI: include a viewport indicator (e.g., a small label showing window width) for layout-dependent questions
No CLI argument parsing unless the prototype genuinely needs parameters
No tests for the prototype itself
No logging configuration; print() is fine
No type annotations beyond what runs without errors
No README sections beyond what's in substep 3d
If the prototype works on the first run and answers the question, do not iterate to "improve" it
